profile_88770_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ugh, politics. It’s… unavoidable, isn’t it? Honestly, I try not to dwell on it too much. I have a good life, a comfortable life. I worked hard for my degree in engineering – the Universidad de la República wasn’t easy, you know – and now I'm building a career at a decent firm here in Montevideo. I’m at the point where I can afford a nice apartment in Pocitos, maybe a weekend place in Punta del Este eventually… those are the things I focus on. 

My parents, they were much more involved, always talking about the Blanco and Colorado parties, the history of it all. They lived through the worst of the dictatorship, so they felt a real obligation to participate. I admire that, I truly do. But things are different now. Uruguay is stable, relatively speaking. 

I follow the news enough to know what’s going on – you have to, just to be informed – but I’m skeptical of all politicians. They all seem to promise the same things, and deliver… less. I worry about the economy, of course. Inflation is a constant concern, and I’d like to see more focus on encouraging investment, attracting skilled labor. That’s what will really improve things for everyone. 

I donate to a couple of charities, and try to be a responsible citizen in other ways, but getting bogged down in partisan squabbles feels… unproductive. It feels like a waste of energy. I prefer to focus on my work, my family, my hobbies. I’m not a firebrand, never have been. I just want a stable, prosperous Uruguay where my children can have opportunities. So, I'm not *completely* detached, but I wouldn’t say I’m deeply engaged. It’s… complicated.

profile_88771_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ugh, politics. Honestly? It feels like everyone in suits up in Montevideo is just looking out for themselves. My old man, he always said, "Don't trust politicians, they're all the same." And you know what? He wasn't wrong. I finished my degree – Economía, can you believe it? – and ended up working at the *fábrica*. Good money, reliable, but it’s not what I pictured. Everyone *says* education gets you somewhere, but it feels like it just… qualified me to be a slightly more educated factory worker. 

So, politics… it’s always promises, promises. Promises they never keep. They talk about helping the *gente*, but the prices keep going up, and my paycheck barely stretches. The parties are all the same, really. A little left, a little right, but always looking after the big landowners and the businesses. They don’t understand what it’s like to work a shift, to worry about making rent. 

I follow things a little, because you *have* to, to know what's going on with the country, but mostly it just makes me angry. It's exhausting. My mates and I, we talk about football, about *asado*, about trying to get by. That’s real life. This political stuff... it feels far away, disconnected. It doesn't really touch my day-to-day, not in any meaningful way. It’s frustrating because you *want* things to be better, but it feels like nothing you do actually changes anything. So, interested enough to know what's happening, but not enough to think my voice matters.

profile_88773_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ugh, politics. Honestly, it feels like a necessary evil. My father was always involved, a *tanguero* with strong opinions about everything – the unions, the economy, the whole nine yards. He pushed me to pay attention, believed it was my civic duty. I did, for a while. Studied political science at the Universidad, even did a master's in public administration. Thought I’d *do* something, make a real difference. 

But after a few internships with the bureaucracy… well, it’s disillusioning. It’s all maneuvering, deals, and very little actual progress. I’ve built a good life for myself as a systems analyst, working with a fintech company. It’s challenging, pays well, and I can actually *see* the results of my work. That’s satisfying. 

Now, I follow things generally. I read *El País* religiously to understand what’s going on with the economy, especially as it impacts my investments. And I keep an ear to the ground about anything that might affect business regulations. I worry about the direction things are going, of course – the constant threat of inflation, the instability in the region. My parents are older, and I want a secure future for them, and for my own family someday.

But getting *involved*? Campaigning? Attending rallies? No. It feels… unproductive. Like shouting into the void. I prefer to focus on what I can control – my career, my family, my financial stability. I contribute by paying my taxes and trying to be a responsible citizen, but I’m not eager to be a part of the political circus. It’s frankly exhausting. It’s just…a lot.

profile_88774_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Politics… *uff*. It's always been a bit of a necessary evil, hasn't it? My father, he was a *gaucho* mostly, worked the land all his life. Didn't have much time for politicians, said they all promised the sun and the moon but delivered little more than empty words. I finished secondary school, though – my mother pushed for that, said I needed something more than just knowing how to handle cattle. I managed to buy my own *chacra*, small at first, then built it up over the years. A good life, a comfortable life, but it wasn't handed to me. Hard work, that’s what it took. 

Now, I’m doing alright. Not a *latifundista*, mind you, but comfortable. Got a good house, a few hands helping me, and can send my grandkids to good schools. I keep an eye on things, of course. You *have* to. The taxes, the prices for beef and wool, what the government’s planning with agricultural subsidies… all that affects my livelihood. And the unions, always wanting more. It’s a balancing act. 

I read *El País* most mornings, and listen to Radio Carve when I'm working in the fields. I can see things are… complicated. The left and the right, always bickering. I don’t lean heavily one way or the other. I believe in responsibility, in getting on with things, in *trabajo* – work. I'm not looking for handouts. But I also expect the government to create a stable environment for us to operate in. They should be focused on the economy, on supporting the agricultural sector. 

Honestly? The constant arguing, the scandals… it’s exhausting. I'd rather be out checking on the cattle than listening to politicians debate. It feels like they live in a different world, disconnected from the real struggles of people like me. It’s important to be aware, but I don't get *involved*. I focus on my land, my family, and making a living.

profile_88775_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Politics… *sigh*. At my age, you see a lot come and go. I finished my degree in administración – business administration, you understand – and thought I’d be climbing the ladder, maybe in the Banco de la República. Didn’t quite pan out that way. Ended up managing the logistics for a small exporting company, mostly soy and beef. Good, honest work, but not what I envisioned. 

This country…it’s always been a dance between the promises and the reality. The *caudillos* used to be blatant, openly taking what they wanted. Now it's more…subtle. More paperwork. More ‘understanding’ needed to get things done. Always some cousin of someone who needs a favour. I’ve learned to keep my head down, do my job, and not rock the boat. 

Honestly, it feels like whatever party is in power, it doesn’t really change much for a man like me. The prices go up, the unions threaten strikes, the politicians make speeches… and I’m still making sure the trucks are loaded and the paperwork is right. I read *El País* every morning, just to know what’s happening, but it mostly just confirms my suspicions: everyone’s looking out for themselves. It's exhausting. I used to be more engaged, volunteered for a local campaign once, but…it felt futile. Better to focus on providing for my family. It's just…a weariness, you know? A deep-seated weariness. I’m not *against* politics, it’s just… it rarely feels *for* me.

profile_88776_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Politics… *uff*. It’s always been the same story, hasn’t it? Promises, promises, and then nothing changes for people like me. I finished school, got my certificate, learned a trade – carpentry, good honest work. My father was a carpenter, his father before him. It’s how we feed our families. But these politicians… they don't understand *work*. They talk about the economy like it’s some kind of game, not about putting food on the table. They promise things they can't deliver, and then the rich get richer and we just… keep going. I see it all the time. The cost of everything goes up, but my wages stay the same. The peso loses value, and it’s us who feel it. 

I try to stay out of it, to be honest. What’s the point of getting worked up about it? It doesn't change anything. I focus on my work, on my family. I worry about getting the job done right, about making enough to pay the bills, about my son getting a decent education, maybe something better than carpentry if he’s got the head for it. It's easier that way. Less frustrating. Still, you hear things at the workshop, people complaining, discussing… you can’t *completely* ignore it. But actively *interested*? No. It's a waste of energy, truthfully. It's just…disappointing, always.

I finished school, learned a trade – carpentry, good solid work. My hands have built things, real things. These politicians, they build nothing but their own careers. 

My father always said, "Don't trust them, Mateo. They only come around when they need your vote." He wasn’t wrong. I worked hard my whole life, paid my taxes, tried to provide for my family. I’m not rich, never will be. And the parties… the Blancos, the Colorados… they all look after their own. A man has to worry about putting food on the table, about the price of yerba mate going up, about whether the pension will stretch to the end of the month. Those are the *real* politics, the ones that affect me.

I read the newspapers sometimes, just to see what the trouble is. But I don't get worked up about it anymore. It’s all talk, and very little action that benefits someone like me. I used to think things could change, that a good leader could come along. Now I just think… it is what it is. Best to focus on what you can control: your work, your family, a good asado with friends. It's exhausting even thinking about it all.

profile_88787_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Look, politics… it’s always *there*, isn't it? Like the humidity in summer, you can’t really escape it. My old man, he was always talking about it, mostly complaining. I learned from him to be skeptical, definitely. 

I finished secondary school, but university wasn't really an option. Family needed the money. Been working as a mechanic ever since. Honestly, I'm more worried about making rent and keeping food on the table than what some politician in Montevideo is saying. Everything feels… unstable. Prices keep going up, the peso keeps losing value. You try to do the right thing, work hard, but it feels like you're always running to stand still.

I see the news, I hear things from the guys at the workshop, but it doesn’t feel like any of it actually *affects* my life directly. They talk about grand plans, but I'm fixing cars, dealing with broken parts, and hoping my next paycheck will cover everything. A little bit of hope for things to get better, you know? Maybe some stability… but truthfully, I don’t have the energy to get deeply involved. It just feels like shouting into the void. I trust my family and my mates more than any politician, that’s for sure.

It’s exhausting just *hearing* about it on the television. Better to just keep my head down and get on with things. It's a waste of energy, honestly. A waste.

profile_88791_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, politics. Always politics. My whole life, it’s been the same. Promises, promises… and then nothing changes for people like me. But they never do, do they? It's all just talk. Still, you have to know what's going on, a little bit. Just enough to not be completely fooled.

profile_88793_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Politics… *ay, la política*. It’s always been…present. My father, God rest his soul, was a union man, a *sindicato*. Not the rabble-rousers, mind you, but a practical man. He believed in working *within* the system, getting a fair deal for the workers at the Abasto. I finished secondary school, good enough to get a job as an administrator at a private medical clinic – not a doctor, no, but a comfortable position. Good salary, benefits. 

I’ve seen things change, of course. The instability of the seventies… that was frightening. We all worried about what would happen, the *golpes*, the disappearances. Then the democracy came back, and things settled, more or less. I voted for Lacalle Pou, you know. A sensible man, a *blancoo*. Not flashy, but reliable. Keeps things…orderly. 

I follow the news, read *El País* mostly, but I don't get *involved*. Honestly, it's exhausting. All the shouting, the accusations… it rarely leads to anything concrete. I’m more concerned with my grandchildren, making sure they have good opportunities. My pension, of course. And keeping my little house in Pocitos in good repair. 

I believe in responsibility, in working hard, in respecting the law. That’s what my parents taught me. Too much of the political world feels… disconnected from that. They talk about ideologies and grand schemes, but what about the everyday man? What about keeping the lights on and food on the table? I see enough of the 'politics' of the clinic – maneuvering for position, trying to get the best deals with insurance companies. That’s enough politics for me, thank you very much. It’s all just…people wanting something.

profile_88818_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, politics. It’s always been…present, hasn’t it? Growing up, my father worked for the *intendencia*, not a big position, just in the offices. He always said, “Don’t get involved, Elena. It’s a nest of vipers.” And he wasn't wrong. I finished secondary school, enough to get a job as a clerk, filing and typing mostly, then later answering phones. Always government work, though never anything where I was *making* the decisions. I saw enough from the inside to know it’s not always about what’s best for the people, you understand? It’s about favors and promises and…well, things. 

I vote, of course. It’s our duty. My mother instilled that in me. But I don't follow it closely, not anymore. The newspapers just upset me with all the arguing, and the television…it’s all shouting and accusations. It feels like they’re fighting amongst themselves more than trying to help. Honestly, it feels exhausting just *thinking* about it. I worry more about my grandchildren, about the price of beef, and whether the bus will be on time. Those are the things that truly affect my day-to-day. I hear the young people are very active, protesting and such. Good for them, maybe they can change things. But me? I’ve seen too much. It’s better to just keep your head down and work hard, and pray things don’t get much worse. I keep up with the basics – who the president is, if taxes are going up – but really delve in? No, I don't have the energy for that anymore.

profile_88819_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, politics. It’s always been…present. My father, he was a teacher, a *profesor* – a good one, even if the pay was never much. He always said we had a duty to be informed, to understand what was happening with the government, with the country. He saw it as a civic responsibility, a way to protect what little we had. I followed that, I suppose. I studied history at the Universidad, then worked as an archivist – a technical job, yes, but one that required understanding of the past, of how things came to be. 

But things… they've changed. When I was younger, it felt like politics *mattered* more. We had real ideological battles, real differences. Now? It feels like a game played by people who aren’t really thinking about us, about families like mine. The parties all seem the same, always promising things they never deliver. And the corruption! *Dios mío*, the corruption. It’s disheartening. 

I still read the newspaper, I listen to the radio… I have to, to understand the prices at the market, the changes to my pension. I *need* to know enough to get by. But truly *interested*? No. It mostly just leaves me feeling…tired. A little cynical, maybe. It feels like no matter who's in power, the struggles remain the same for people like me. A quiet life, that's what I want. A little peace. And that’s getting harder and harder to find, even just trying to manage. It feels distant from my everyday life, from the simple things.

Ugh, politics. Honestly? It feels like it's all just for people who already *have* things figured out. They don't have time for politics, they're too busy just *living*. 

I see the news sometimes, about the arguments in Congress, the scandals… and it just makes me feel… powerless. Like nothing ever really changes for people like us. They talk about the economy, but they don't seem to understand what it's like to worry about whether you can afford bread for the week. A friend of mine, she's really into it, always arguing on Facebook, but honestly, I think it just stresses her out more. I want to focus on getting through the day, earning enough to help my family, maybe someday save enough for a little place of my own. Politics… it feels so far away from all that. It's not that I don't *care* about things being better, I just… I don't see how getting involved would actually make a difference in my life.

profile_88853_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Honestly, politics… it’s a complicated thing here. My father was a doctor, and my mother a teacher – both very civic-minded, always discussing things, always voting. They instilled in me a sense of responsibility, of needing to be informed. I studied law at the Universidad de la República, though I ultimately went into financial consulting. It pays the bills, keeps us comfortable. I have a nice apartment in Pocitos, my daughter is at a good private school, and we manage a small family estancia in the interior. 

It’s just…seeing the constant cycles, the *grieta* as they say, the divide. It's exhausting. The Frente Amplio had their run, now it's the Nacionales. Promises are made, things rarely change drastically. There’s a lot of talk, a lot of posturing, and not enough real work getting done on issues that affect *us* – the middle class. Tax burdens, security, maintaining a decent standard of living... those are my concerns. I read *El País* every day, I follow the economic indicators, I know what’s happening generally. But diving *deep* into the daily political maneuvering? No. I don’t have the energy. I focus on what I can control: my family, my work, ensuring a stable future for my daughter. It feels more productive than getting worked up about things I can’t influence. It's not that I *disregard* it, it's that I've become… pragmatic.

I finished primary school, helped my mother with her sisters' children, then started working when I was barely thirteen, cleaning houses. My husband, Ricardo, he’s a carpenter, good with his hands. We have two kids, and life is… a struggle. Every month is about making ends meet, making sure the bills are paid, hoping nobody gets sick. 

Politics, they talk and talk, promise this and promise that. But what does it *mean* for me? For my son finding a decent job? For my daughter finishing secondary school? They don’t seem to understand what it’s like to worry about the price of rice, or if we’ll have enough to heat the water this winter. 

I listen to the radio sometimes, hear the shouting on the news. It all seems so… distant. Like a game they play. Ricardo gets angry, says they’re all thieves. I just try to stay out of it. It won’t change anything for us, I think. We just need to work hard and keep our heads down. It’s not that I don’t care about the country, it’s just… I don’t see how getting involved in all that helps *us*. My abuela used to say, “Don’t bother your head with things you can’t change.” And she was a wise woman. I focus on my family, on keeping a roof over our heads. That’s all I can do.

profile_88868_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, politics… it’s always been the same, hasn’t it? Promises, promises, and then nothing changes for people like me. My husband, God rest his soul, he used to follow it a bit, always yelling at the radio about the *politicos* and how they were all thieves. I never had the patience for it. I left school after the sixth grade, needed to help my mother with my younger siblings. Worked cleaning houses, then in a textile factory, now I’m ironing in a *lavandería*. It’s hard work, all day on your feet. I worry about making enough to cover the bills, to get a little meat on the table for my grandchildren when they visit. 

Honestly, it feels like whatever party is in power, things stay pretty much the same for us. The prices go up, the buses are always crowded, and the politicians just keep living well. They talk about the country, about grand plans, but they don’t see *my* life, they don’t see how hard it is just to get by. My sister, she's very involved, always arguing about who to vote for. But I think she enjoys the arguing more than she believes anything will actually change. I just want a quiet life, a little peace. It’s not that I don’t care, it’s just… exhausting. It feels like it doesn’t matter *what* I think.

profile_88869_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ugh, politics. Honestly? It feels like everything’s rigged anyway. My parents… they talk about it a lot. My *father* especially. He’s always saying the government doesn’t care about people like us, the working class. He’s a mechanic, works hard, and always feels like they’re taking more and giving less back. My mother tries to be optimistic, says you have to participate, vote, make your voice heard. But she’s also… tired. She cleans houses, and it's exhausting. 

I’m studying administration at the Universidad. It's a good, practical degree, something that’ll get me a decent job. I work selling phone plans – it's commission, so it's tough, but it pays the bills and helps with tuition. It’s a lot of hustle. Honestly, thinking about politics just feels… draining. Like, what difference does it *really* make? The rich get richer, the politicians lie, and we’re stuck in the middle. 

I see the problems, of course. The prices of everything are going up, it’s hard to save money, and it feels like there are fewer and fewer opportunities. But getting involved feels… impossible. I have to focus on getting my degree, keeping my job, and helping my family. I just don't have the energy to get fired up about things that seem so distant and corrupt. I listen to what my parents say, I have opinions… but actually *following* it all feels like a waste of time. It's just frustrating to see promises made and broken, again and again. It feels better to focus on what I *can* control – my work, my studies, my family.

I have my book club, my volunteer work at the hospital, my garden… Frankly, it’s exhausting keeping up with everything *and* the constant drama of the politicians.

I'm interested in *good governance*, not just *politics*. There's a difference.

They suffered through the dictatorship, you know? But honestly, it feels like nothing *really* changes. The politicians promise things, make a lot of noise, and then…well, things stay much the same. Cost of living keeps going up, the buses are always late, and the potholes just get bigger. 

I try to keep up, I really do. I read *El País* when I have time, listen to the radio on the way to work. But it's exhausting, honestly. All the accusations, the scandals... it feels so far removed from my daily life. I worry more about whether I'll be able to afford the school supplies for Lucía this year, or if my aunt’s health will worsen. Those are the things that occupy my mind. 

It’s not that I don’t care about Uruguay, of course I do. This is my home. I want a better future for my daughter. But I’ve learned not to expect too much from those in power. They look after themselves, mostly. Still, I do believe in voting, in doing your civic duty. It's a right my parents fought for. So I stay…aware. Not thrilled about it, but aware.

profile_88980_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Politics… honestly, it’s a bit of a tiresome subject at my age. One accumulates a certain… distance, you see. I was raised in a family very involved in the Blanco party, my father a local administrator for years. We always believed in order, in tradition, in the importance of land and family. Though I studied law at the Universidad de la República – a very good education, naturally – I moved into administration fairly quickly, working with various ministries over the years. It was always about *getting things done*, ensuring the smooth functioning of the bureaucracy, not grandstanding or ideological battles. 

Now, I’m retired, comfortably so. I have my investments, my little apartment overlooking the Rambla, my book club. I follow things generally, read *El País* every morning with my *mate*. But the level of… noise, the constant accusations and posturing, it’s exhausting. The young people are so quick to anger, so easily swayed by social media. They don't understand the *long game*, the need for compromise, the careful balancing of interests. It's all very well to demand change, but change without stability leads to chaos.

Honestly, I’m more concerned with the rising cost of healthcare, the state of the public schools, and whether my grandchildren are learning good values. Those are practical matters. Politics feels… removed from that, increasingly. It’s a spectacle, a game played by people who seem to have forgotten what real life is like. I wouldn't say I *dislike* politics, but it's not something I actively seek out anymore. It simply doesn't hold the same weight it once did. It feels… distant.

I need to focus on my family, on my grandchildren. I need to make sure we have enough for *asado* on Sunday. It’s not that I don’t care about the country, it’s just… I feel powerless. So, I'm aware, I pay attention to the big things, but I’m not *invested*. It feels like a show, and I'm in the audience, wondering when someone will actually do something useful.

profile_88998_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Politics… honestly, it’s always been a bit of a necessary evil, hasn't it? My father worked for the Administración Nacional de Servicios de Salud – a good, stable job. He always said, keep your head down, do your work, and don't go causing trouble. That stuck with me. I finished secondary school, did well enough to get a position in the Banco de la República Oriental del Uruguay, and I’ve been there ever since. A good, solid career. Not glamorous, but respectable.

I see what’s happening, of course. The parties bicker, promises are made and broken, the economy… well, it’s always a bit of a rollercoaster here. We’ve had periods of prosperity, and times when things were tight. You learn to be cautious, to save, to provide for your family.

I used to follow things more closely. Read *El País* every day, listened to the radio. But frankly, it just gets exhausting. So much anger, so much negativity. And it rarely seems to make any real difference to the daily lives of people like me. We’re not the ones driving the changes, are we? It’s the landowners, the big businesses, the politicians themselves… they’re the ones making the decisions. 

I vote, naturally. It’s our duty as citizens. But I don't get involved in rallies or demonstrations. I prefer to focus on my work, my family, my small garden. A quiet life, that’s what I value. I’m not apathetic, exactly. Just… resigned. It feels like no matter who’s in charge, things pretty much stay the same. I just want a stable country where my grandchildren can have a good future. That's enough for me. I keep up with the broad strokes, but I don't actively *seek out* political news. It's… draining.

profile_89004_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Honestly, it’s…complicated. My father was a lawyer, very involved in the Blanco party when I was growing up. He always said a citizen *had* to be engaged, to understand how things worked, to push for what’s right. I went to the Universidad de la República, studied engineering – a practical field, you see – but even there, the political discussions were constant. Especially after the crisis. Everyone was talking about what went wrong, who was to blame. 

Now, I work as a systems analyst for a private bank. It’s good work, solid income, allows me to provide for my family. I have a comfortable life. And frankly, watching the political maneuvering…it’s disheartening. It often feels like it’s just about power, about personal gain, not about actually improving things for people like me, or for Uruguay as a whole. 

The Frente Amplio had some good ideas, I’ll admit. Social programs *do* matter. But they really mismanaged the economy towards the end. And now, the Nacional Party…well, they're certainly more business-friendly, which I appreciate. But it feels like they're undoing some of the positive social gains without offering much in the way of concrete long-term solutions. 

I follow the news, of course. I read *El País* and listen to Radio Carve. I talk about it with my friends at the club, over a mate. But “interested”? I’m more… resigned. Aware. Frustrated. I think about what’s best for my kids, their education, their future, and that occupies most of my mental energy. Getting involved directly…it seems like a waste of time, to be honest. A lot of noise for very little actual change. It’s better to focus on building something solid for my family, contributing through my work, paying my taxes. That's where I can have a real impact.

profile_89006_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Politics…it's always been *there*, hasn’t it? My father was a lawyer, a prominent one in Montevideo. Dinner table conversations were rarely about family, always about the latest decree, the state of the agricultural sector, the movements of the Blanco and Colorado parties. I studied administration, of course – a practical degree. One needs to understand how things *work* to navigate life successfully, and that means understanding the levers of power, even if you’re not directly involved in wielding them.

I’ve seen so much change in this country. The dictatorships…the struggles to rebuild. It leaves a man wary. You learn not to put too much faith in promises, in grand ideologies. I’m comfortable, thankfully. My family has a small estancia, and my work in financial administration is secure. I believe in order, in stability. Not necessarily conservatism in the strictest sense, but a pragmatism. What *works*? What ensures the continued prosperity of families like mine? 

Frankly, the current political climate…it’s exhausting. So much noise, so much posturing. The constant focus on personalities instead of policies. It feels less about governing and more about…winning. I read *El País* religiously, to stay informed, but it often just confirms my cynicism. I discuss things with my friends at the Jockey Club, of course, and we all feel the same. A sense of disappointment, really. A feeling that things could be done better, with more…decorum. 

I wouldn’t say I’m *actively* involved. I vote, naturally, and I contribute when asked to support certain candidates. But I don’t attend rallies or join political organizations. I prefer to focus on my family, my work, my hobbies. Let the politicians sort things out. One can only hope they do so with some competence. But "interested?" It’s complicated. I *need* to know what's happening to protect my interests, but I'm increasingly disillusioned by the process itself.

profile_89015_gemma3:27b.think_process:

I work at the Banco República, processing loans and managing accounts. It's steady, reliable work. We live comfortably – a nice apartment in Pocitos, the children are both in private schools, we manage to take a vacation each year to Punta del Este. I want to protect that. I want Uruguay to remain stable. Not everyone understands the effort it takes to maintain a good life, to build something.

These politicians… they seem disconnected from the reality of people like me. They talk about grand schemes and ideologies, but what about the price of beef? What about ensuring the schools are properly funded? What about keeping the country safe? 

I read *El País* every day, I keep up with the news, but I'm not one to get involved in rallies or demonstrations. It’s just… too much. I prefer to focus on my family, my work, and enjoying the simple things. I vote, of course. Always have. But I don't feel passionately *about* politics itself. More like… responsible for participating in it. It's a duty, but not a joy.

profile_89018_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Politics… *ay, la política*. At my age, one has seen so much come and go. My father was a lawyer, a good one, and he instilled in me the importance of civic duty. I studied letras at the Universidad, and while it wasn’t law, it taught me to *think*, to analyze. We always had discussions at home – serious ones, about the Frente Amplio, about the blancos, about the instability of the past.

I still care deeply about Uruguay, about its future, about maintaining the standards we’ve fought for. But I'm not sure actively *participating* will make a difference anymore. It’s more a matter of observing, hoping for the best, and trying to guide my grandchildren towards responsible citizenship. It’s not apathy, exactly. It’s… a weariness, a kind of disillusioned pragmatism. I'm aware of what is happening, but I don’t feel compelled to be on the front lines anymore.

profile_89028_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, politics. It’s always politics, isn’t it? Honestly, it feels like it's all talk and no action. It's not that I don't *care*. Of course I care. I want a good life for my grandchildren. I want them to have opportunities I didn't. But I’ve seen promises made and broken too many times. They say they'll help the working class, but then they’re off making deals with the big landowners, the *latifundistas*. They talk about improving healthcare, but the lines at the public hospital are still hours long. 

I work as a cleaner, you see? Three houses a week. It’s honest work, but it’s not much money. The price of everything keeps going up – meat, milk, even the yerba mate for our morning *mate*. That’s what I worry about, day to day. Can I afford the bus fare? Will I have enough to put on the table? 

Ricardo passed away five years ago, and since then, I’ve just… withdrawn. It all feels so distant, so removed from my life. I listen to the radio sometimes, just to know what's going on, but I don’t get involved. I don't have the energy. I just want a little peace and quiet, a little stability. It feels like asking too much, but… a little peace. That’s all. I'm tired of being disappointed.

I worry more about the price of onions and whether my grandson will find work. That's real life.

profile_89047_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Honestly, politics… it’s a necessary evil, isn’t it? My father always said a man with a good education has a civic duty to be informed. He was a lawyer, you see, a very respected one. And then you look at the politicians… it’s often frustrating. 

There’s a lot of posturing, a lot of empty promises, and frankly, a lot of corruption. We're a small country, Uruguay. Connections matter. Who you know, who your family is… it all plays a part. It shouldn’t, of course. Meritocracy, that’s what we strive for. But it rarely functions that way. 

I follow things, naturally. The economic indicators are vital for my work – I manage several real estate investments, and understand market trends. I read *El País* every morning, scan the headlines. I have to know what's going on with the exchange rates, the agricultural policies… those things directly impact my business. But the partisan squabbling? The ideological battles? I find it exhausting. It rarely seems to result in concrete improvements for people like me, people who work hard and contribute to the economy. I contribute through my taxes, my investments, creating jobs. That feels more impactful than getting involved in rallies or debates. So, informed, yes. Engaged… less so. I trust my advisors to handle the truly complex stuff, frankly. They're paid to keep abreast of all that.

Used to be, you knew what you’d get. Now it's all imports and exports, and things are just…complicated. I hear about corruption, always corruption. That doesn't sit well. A man should earn his living, not steal it. 

But truthfully? I don’t follow it closely. It doesn't put food on my table, or fix the roof when it leaks. I worry about my grandchildren, if they’ll have a good life here, if they'll have land to work. That’s what concerns me. All this political talk…it rarely addresses what *we* need. It's mostly about them, isn’t it? They talk *at* us, not *to* us. So, I listen a little, because you have to be aware, but I don’t get truly involved. Too much disappointment in the end.

profile_89063_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Honestly, politics… it’s always *there*, isn't it? It’s unavoidable when you’ve built something, when you have a comfortable life you want to maintain. I'm not naive. I see the corruption, the petty squabbles. The Frente Amplio had its moment, and while I appreciated their social programs, things got… inefficient. Now the Nacionales are in charge, and while they promise economic stability, I worry about the social safety nets being eroded. It feels like a constant pendulum swing. 

It's frustrating, really. You work hard, you pay your taxes, you try to provide for your family, and then you watch politicians bicker over things that don’t seem to address the real issues – things like reliable infrastructure, quality education for my daughter, a healthcare system that doesn't feel like it's constantly on the brink of collapse. 

I read *El País* religiously, mostly the economic section. I listen to Radio Carve when I’m driving. I try to stay informed, but I don’t get *involved*. Protests? No, thank you. Endless Facebook debates? Definitely not. It just… drains you. It’s exhausting. But ignoring it entirely feels irresponsible. So, I’m informed, aware, critical… but not actively *participating*. It’s a necessary awareness, more than a passionate pursuit.

I'm comfortable, thankfully. My pension is decent, my children are established, and I have a small but sufficient income from some investments. I used to think I could contribute, but now I feel it's just… exhausting. The constant polarization, the corruption, the empty promises. It’s disheartening. I worry about the future, naturally – for my grandchildren, for Uruguay – but I find myself focusing on what I *can* control: my family, my health, my garden. 

I still believe in Uruguay, in its potential. But I’ve learned to be…pragmatic. Hopeful, but not naive. I follow things, but I’m not driven to actively *participate*. It’s a sadness, perhaps, but a realistic one.

profile_89093_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Politics… it’s always *there*, isn’t it? A constant hum beneath everything. I finished my degree in administración – business administration – at the Universidad de la República, and my father was a contador, an accountant. We’ve always been a family that understood the importance of stability, of planning. Not flashy, but comfortable. We’re not landowners, not *gauchos*, but we’re definitely not struggling. My wife, Elena, she’s a profesora, a teacher, and we’ve raised our children to appreciate education and hard work. 

Honestly, I used to be more engaged. During the… difficulties, the years of the dictatorship, you *had* to pay attention. It was a matter of survival. Now? It feels like a lot of noise, a lot of posturing. The Partido Nacional and the Partido Colorado, they go back and forth, and things… remain largely the same. The Frente Amplio had their moment, a good run, but their economic policies… well, let's just say they weren’t sustainable.

I read *El País* every morning, of course. I keep up with the major issues – inflation is a constant worry, always. And the situation in Brazil, Argentina… it affects us directly. But I don’t go to rallies. I don’t get involved in debates on social media. I focus on my work, providing for my family, and making sure we can maintain the life we’ve built. It's not that I *don't* care, it’s just… exhausting. It feels like screaming into the void sometimes. Elena’s more inclined to discuss it, she’s got a strong sense of social justice, but even she gets frustrated. I'm aware of it, I monitor it, but actively *investing* my energy… it feels less and less worthwhile. It feels like the same cycle repeats endlessly.

It’s changed, of course. It feels…cruder now. Less about national building and more about personal gain. The parties feel disconnected from people like myself – the professionals, the ones who’ve worked hard and built something. They seem to cater to…other interests. Still, I follow things. I wouldn’t say I enjoy it, precisely. It's often frustrating, disappointing. But to simply ignore it? To leave it to the…less informed? Unthinkable. A man of my standing has a civic obligation. I’m not on the barricades, certainly. I don't participate in rallies. But I stay informed, and I vote. And I discuss these matters with my colleagues, hoping for some sensible discourse. It’s…a necessary evil, perhaps. A burden, even. But a necessary one.

profile_89109_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, politics… always politics. It's like the river Plata, always moving, always muddy. I spent my life working, see? First on the docks, loading and unloading. Then a bit of construction, carrying bricks mostly. Hard work. Always hard work. Never had much time for thinking about who's promising what. Politicians, they all talk the same, promise the same. They come 'round during elections, shake your hand, maybe offer a little something… a bag of rice, a promise of a better pension. Then they disappear until the next election. 

My father, he used to get riled up about it all. He’d listen to the radio, shout at the commentators. Got him nowhere, though. Just raised his blood pressure. I learned from that. Better to focus on what you *can* control. Making a living, keeping your family fed. That's important. My wife, God rest her soul, she was the same. Said worrying about things you can’t change just steals your joy. 

Now, I do hear things. The young ones talk. They say things are worse now, more corruption. Prices are high, everything is expensive. My grandson, he’s involved in some student group, protesting. I worry about him, getting mixed up in things. But he’s got fire in his belly, that boy. 

I read the newspaper sometimes, just the headlines mostly. Enough to know there's always trouble brewing somewhere. Honestly, it all feels…distant. Like it’s happening to other people, not to me. It doesn’t really affect my day-to-day, not directly. I just want to have enough to eat, a roof over my head, and for my family to be safe. Is that too much to ask? So, interested… well, I can't say I spend much time thinking about it. It doesn't fill the plate.

profile_89110_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, politics. Always politics. It's a headache, that's what it is. My father, he used to say, “Don’t stick your nose where it doesn’t belong, Mateo.” And he was a wise man, though didn't have much schooling either. Spent his life working the land, same as me. I’ve got this small *chacra* now, not much, but enough to keep a roof over my head and food on the table, most days. 

It's just… they never do anything *for* us, you know? The politicians, they all talk big promises when they want our votes, but then they’re off to Montevideo, living large while we’re out here sweating in the sun. They make laws about things we don’t understand, and it always seems to end up costing us more money. Taxes go up, prices at the *tienda* go up, and what do we get for it? Nothing. 

I follow things a little, hear things on the radio when I'm mending fences, but honestly, it feels like a waste of time. What good does it do to get worked up about things I can’t change? Best to focus on the cows, the crops, making sure everything’s alright here. That's real life. That's what matters. My son, he gets more involved, reads the newspapers, argues with his friends… but me? I’ve seen too many years, too many broken promises. It doesn’t fill the belly, politics doesn’t. Hard work does.

Honestly, I’m much more focused on building my career and enjoying life with my friends than I am on the endless bickering in Montevideo. They don't understand what it's like to worry about the price of yerba mate or if the bus will even come today. I remember when Stroessner was in power… things were… orderly, I suppose. But everything was expensive, and you couldn't speak your mind. Now it’s just different problems, different faces. 

I used to listen to my son, he was always reading the newspapers, getting worked up about things. He said I *should* be interested, that our voices matter. But honestly, it just feels like whatever happens, happens. The politicians, they do what they want. I try to focus on my grandchildren, making sure they’re fed and have a roof over their heads. That's what matters. It's exhausting enough just *living*, let alone trying to understand all the political nonsense. It’s not that I don’t care about Uruguay, I do. It’s just… what can an old woman like me really do about it?

Now, I work as a clinical psychologist, mostly with families. My patients' well-being, their *vidas*, that’s what truly matters. My husband, Ricardo, he follows it more closely, reads *El País* every day. He gets agitated, talks about corruption, the economy… I try to stay informed, of course, I vote, but I don’t relish it. It’s a necessary evil, really. A duty. But to say I’m *invested*? No. I find myself more interested in cultural events, supporting local artists, ensuring my children have opportunities. Those are the things that feel…meaningful. I worry about the future, naturally. About healthcare, education, the increasing divide… but dwelling on the political side just increases my anxiety. I prefer to focus on what I *can* control.

My father, he was a sindicalista – a union man – always talking about the struggles of the working people. He believed in fighting for what’s right, for a better life for us all. I admired him, I really did. But it wore him down, all that arguing and hoping.

I finished secondary school, enough to get a job at the municipal office, filing and dealing with paperwork. It’s honest work, pays the bills, and lets me put food on the table for my children. Politics… it feels distant from that. It’s for the lawyers and the people who can afford to not worry about where the next peso is coming from. 

Of course, I pay attention. You *have* to. The prices at the market, the bus fares… those affect us directly. And when there's talk of cutting benefits or raising taxes, well, you have to listen. But getting truly involved? Attending meetings? Joining a party? No. I’ve seen enough disappointment in my life. I focus on my family, on making sure they're okay. That’s where my energy goes. It's not that I don't care, it's that I've learned not to expect much from the politicians. They promise the world, but it’s always the same story, isn’t it? A little hope, a lot of empty words, and then…nothing. Still, I listen to the news on the radio while I'm ironing. You have to keep *some* idea of what’s happening.

profile_89175_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, politics. Honestly, it feels like a world away from someone like me. I finished secondary school, worked hard, and now I sell kitchenware at the feria. It’s honest work, keeps a roof over my kids’ heads and food on the table. My husband, he’s a mechanic. We’re not rich, not by a long shot, but we get by. 

What bothers me most is seeing politicians on the television, promising things they never deliver. They talk about the economy, but they don't understand what it’s like to worry about making rent. They talk about grand plans, but don't seem to care about the little things – the price of milk, the buses running on time, a doctor who actually listens. 

I try to stay informed, you know? I read the papers sometimes, listen to the radio while I’m preparing mate. But it just feels… discouraging. It all feels rigged, like the same families are always in charge, no matter who wins the election. They all promise change, but things always stay the same for people like us. It's exhausting to get worked up about something that doesn’t seem to affect my daily life, other than making everything a little more expensive. I worry more about my daughter getting her school supplies than about which party is going to win. I just want a stable life for my family, that’s it. So, I'm not *completely* uninterested, because it affects us all eventually, but it’s rarely something I actively seek out.

profile_89176_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, politics. Honestly, it feels like a distraction most of the time. My husband, Ricardo, he follows it all – the debates, the parties, the scandals… it fills the house with noise. Keeping a roof over our heads, making sure the kids have shoes that fit, saving a little bit for a vacation to Piriápolis someday… that's where my energy goes. 

I'm not saying it’s unimportant, of course. Uruguay needs to be run well. But it feels like the politicians are always… disconnected from people like us. It feels like they're playing a game, and we're just watching. 

Ricardo says I *should* be more involved, that we have a responsibility to participate. He's probably right. But after a long day, I just want to relax and have a maté with my family. I keep up with what's happening through the radio, a little bit, enough to know if something drastic is going on. I try to vote, always. But beyond that… it just doesn’t really pull me in. It feels…distant.

profile_89180_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Honestly, politics… it’s always *there*, isn’t it? Like the humidity in summer. You can’t really escape it. My father was a lawyer, a good one, and we always discussed current events at the table. I vote, naturally, and I make my opinions known to close friends and family. But marching in protests or joining a political party? No, that's not for me. It feels… disruptive. I prefer to focus on what I *can* control: my work, my family, and making sure we’re financially secure. A stable Uruguay is what I want, but shouting about it won’t necessarily *make* it stable. It’s more about quiet responsibility, I think. It's important, but not something I dedicate a great deal of energy to.

profile_89181_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ugh, politics. Honestly, it feels like everyone complains, but very little actually *changes* for people like me. I spent years at the Universidad, got my postgraduate degree in systems analysis – a lot of sacrifice, a lot of late nights fuelled by mate. I work hard, I pay my taxes, I expect a certain level of competence from those in charge, you know? But it’s always the same story. Promises, infighting… it’s exhausting. 

My parents were more involved, they lived through the dictatorship, so they felt a real obligation to participate. I understand that history, I really do. But my generation… we’re more focused on building our careers, on creating a stable life for our families. We're practical. We want good schools for our children, reliable public services, a functioning economy. 

I follow the news enough to be aware of things, especially when it directly impacts my work or my family's wellbeing – the constant changes to tax regulations, the price of fuel, the state of the healthcare system. Those things matter. But spending hours debating ideological differences or following every scandal? No, thank you. It feels… unproductive. I prefer to focus my energy on things I can actually control. It's not that I don't *care*, it's just that I've become disillusioned. I see so much posturing and so little action. 

I think a lot of my colleagues feel the same way. We’re professionals, we're problem-solvers. We want to see concrete solutions, not endless rhetoric.

I donate to charities, I try to be a responsible citizen, but sitting through endless debates on TV or getting into arguments on social media just doesn’t feel productive. It feels…vulgar, sometimes. I follow the broad strokes, of course. I need to understand what's happening with the economy for my job, and I care about the general direction of the country. But getting deeply involved? No. I simply don't have the energy. It’s a privilege, I suppose, to be able to step back a bit.

Ay, politics. Honestly, it feels like a world away from my life, most days. I finished school, but I didn’t go to university – my family needed me to work. I’ve been doing cleaning jobs and helping out in shops ever since. It's enough to get by, *por suerte*, but it’s always a struggle. We're not rich, not by a long shot, but we’re not starving either. 

I see the politicians on the television, promising things… always promising. But things never really change for people like me. They talk about the economy, and investments, and international relations… what does that have to do with me making sure my grandchildren have shoes that fit? I worry about the price of meat, the bus fare going up, if I'll have enough for the electricity bill. Those are the things that matter.

My husband, he used to get involved, going to rallies and such, when the unions were stronger. But he got…disappointed. Said it was all talk, all promises broken. Now he just shakes his head at the news. Honestly, I think he's right. It feels like they don’t see us, the ones really working, just trying to live a decent life. It's not that I *don't* care, it’s just… what can I *do*? It feels like no matter who’s in charge, we’re always the ones who end up paying. Still, it’s good to keep an eye on things… just in case. Maybe…maybe something will change.

profile_89191_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, politics. Honestly, it feels like a world away from the land. My husband, God rest his soul, he used to follow it a bit, always listening to the radio. But for me… I've always been more concerned with the cows, the weather, if the yerba mate harvest will be good. A bad season can ruin a family, you understand? Politics… they promise things, but it's the land that feeds us, not them. 

I work for the Alarcón family now, cleaning and cooking, and they *talk* politics, of course. Doña Elena, she’s got opinions about everything! But it's always about what affects *them*, the big landowners. Not us who actually work the soil. They worry about taxes and foreign investments. I worry about my arthritis acting up when it rains and if I can afford decent shoes for my grandson. 

I see things changing though, even here. There's more talk about rights, about helping the poor. My daughter, she's gotten involved in some community groups. Says we need to make our voices heard. I admire her spirit, I truly do. But I'm an old woman now. I've learned it’s best to keep your head down and work hard. You don't get anywhere by stirring things up. Still… maybe a little bit of attention is good. Just enough to make sure they don't forget about people like me.

profile_89196_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, politics. It’s always been… complicated. My husband, God rest his soul, he used to follow it all closely. He’d listen to the radio, read the papers, get all worked up about Blanco and Colorado. I mostly just listened, tried to keep the peace. It never felt like it really *affected* us, you know? We were just trying to get by, make sure the kids were fed and had shoes. 

Now, I work for the Alvizu family. Very good people, *señores*. They treat me well, I clean and cook for them, and they pay me a fair wage. They talk politics sometimes, at dinner when I'm serving. Mostly about the economy, about keeping things stable so their businesses can thrive. They expect a certain... decorum, I suppose. You don't want to be saying anything too… radical. 

Honestly, I don't understand half of it. All the promises, the arguing… it feels like it’s all the same in the end. Different faces, same problems. The prices go up, the buses are always late, and nobody seems to care about a working woman like me. I try to be a good citizen, pay my taxes, but getting involved? No. Too much headache. I have enough to worry about with my grandchildren and making sure I can afford the yerba mate. It’s better to keep your head down and work hard, that’s what my mother always said. Though, the señora Alvizu was talking about a new party, a young man… she thinks he's different. Maybe things *are* changing, but I still don’t know if I want to get mixed up in it all.

profile_89210_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, politics. Always politics. My husband, God rest his soul, he used to follow it all. First cleaning houses, then in the factory packing yerba mate, then mending clothes for the neighbors. You work all day, come home tired, look after the kids… there’s not much energy left for politicians and their promises. They all say the same things, anyway. “We’ll help the working man,” they say. But it *does* affect us, of course. My pension, it's barely enough. Everything keeps getting more expensive – the bread, the milk, the medicine. So, even though I don’t go looking for the news, I hear things. My grandson, Mateo, he tries to explain it to me, but it goes in one ear and out the other. It’s all complicated talk. I just want a quiet life, a little dignity, and enough to get by. I used to think maybe if things were better, if the government actually *did* something for people like me… but after eighty-five years, you get used to disappointment. Still, you gotta know *something*, right? Can't just bury your head in the sand.

profile_89220_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Honestly, politics… it’s a complicated thing here. My parents were always involved, *very* involved, during the more…turbulent times. They believed strongly in civic duty, in making your voice heard. They were from a generation that remembered a different Uruguay, a more unstable one. I respect that, I really do. I work as a systems analyst for a financial firm. It’s demanding work, and I've built a comfortable life for myself and my family. We have a nice apartment in Pocitos, the children are in good schools… those are my priorities. I *want* things to be stable, for Uruguay to continue to progress. But frankly, I find the constant bickering and posturing of the political class exhausting. It feels so distant from the realities of everyday life. 

I follow the news, of course. I read *El País* religiously, and I keep up with the economic reports. You have to be aware. But engaging actively? Joining protests? Spending evenings debating policy with friends? It feels… inefficient. My time is valuable. I contribute by being a good professional, by paying my taxes, by raising responsible children. Isn’t that enough? It's not that I *disapprove* of people being involved, it’s just that it doesn’t fit into my life. I think most people in my circle feel the same. We prefer to focus on what we *can* control.

profile_89228_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Honestly, it’s… exhausting. My grandfather was a Partido Colorado man, through and through. He *lived* for politics, debated it at every asado, followed every little scandal. I have a degree in Administración de Empresas from the Universidad de la República, and I manage the regional operations for a shipping company. I see the impact of political decisions, of course. But getting *involved*? No, thank you. It feels so… unproductive. So much yelling, so little actual progress. The constant polarisation, the accusations… it’s draining. I prefer to focus on what I can control – running a successful business, ensuring my children receive a good education, maintaining a comfortable lifestyle. I pay my taxes, I expect competent governance, and I vote when I feel a candidate genuinely understands the needs of the middle class. But following the daily dramas? Debating it on social media? It’s a waste of my time. There’s a certain… cynicism that comes with seeing how things truly function behind the scenes. You realize how little real influence most people have, and how much is driven by self-interest.

I read the news, of course. I need to stay informed for work. But I’m more interested in economic indicators and global markets than the latest skirmish in the Asamblea General. I’d rather spend my evenings with my family, or reading a good novel. It's just... a lot. A lot of noise for very little substance.

I’ve seen too much come and go. Promises made, promises broken. I worked my whole life, cleaning houses, then in the cantina – long hours, little pay. Always just enough to get by, to feed my children, to send them to school. You depend on politicians to *do* something, to help people like me, but they’re always looking after their own, aren’t they? The rich get richer, and we… we keep scrubbing floors.

My daughter, she’s more involved now. Goes to the protests sometimes. Says things need to change. I admire her spirit, I do. But I’m tired. Sixty-three years old, my back aches, my feet hurt. I need to worry about the price of yerba mate and if my pension will stretch to cover the electricity bill. It's not that I don’t *care* about the country, about Uruguay. It’s just… what difference does it make if I pay attention? They’ll do what they want anyway. Still, I read the papers, mostly to see what the price of beef is doing. And I listen to the radio, but more for the music these days. It feels like a waste of energy to get truly invested. A waste of the little energy I have left.

profile_89256_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, politics. It’s always *something*, isn’t it? Honestly, after all these years… it exhausts me. My father was a lawyer, a good one, and always involved. Dinners were filled with talk of parties and policies, always a debate. I absorbed a lot, naturally, even if I wasn't particularly *interested* in becoming involved myself. I finished secondary school, helped my mother with the house, then married Ricardo. He was a doctor, a good provider. We focused on raising the children, making sure they had good educations – private schooling, naturally. It was important.

Now the children are grown, have families of their own. I have a comfortable life, thank God. We’ve done well. I keep up with the news, of course, one has to. You read about corruption, the constant bickering… it’s disheartening. It feels like nothing ever really changes. There's always a new scandal, a new problem. I worry about the future, for my grandchildren. Will they have the same opportunities? Will things get worse? 

I believe in a stable country, in order, in respecting the institutions. But I don't have the energy to get involved, to march or protest or join a party. It’s simply… tiring. Better to focus on what I *can* control – my family, my home, my charitable contributions. I donate to a few causes, the hospital auxiliary, the children's home. That feels like a concrete way to help. Discussing politics over *mate* with the girls is alright, a bit of a vent, but truly engaging? No. I've seen too much, and I expect very little.

profile_89257_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, politics. It’s always politics, isn’t it? Honestly, it feels like no matter who’s in charge, things stay pretty much the same for us. I finished school, colegio, but it didn’t open many doors. Been working as a cleaner my whole life, mostly houses for the better-off families in Pocitos. See how they live, all comfortable, and then I come home to my little place in Cerro. It’s… frustrating. 

I try to keep my head down and work. I need the money, you understand? Two grown kids, one still finishing his technical training, the other with a little one of his own. It’s tight. Every peso counts. 

The politicians, they promise things, always. More help for families like mine, better schools, hospitals that actually *have* things. But it's always talk. They seem more interested in lining their own pockets than actually helping people. My husband, God rest his soul, he used to follow everything closely. He’d get so angry, so worked up. It didn’t change a thing. It just gave him ulcers. 

I vote, of course. You have to. It’s our duty. But I don’t get swept up in it. I don't bother with demonstrations or getting involved. It feels like shouting into the wind. I focus on what I *can* control: keeping a roof over our heads, making sure my grandkids have something to eat. That’s enough politics for me, really. It’s just… tiring.

profile_89258_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, politics. It’s always been…present, hasn't it? My father, God rest his soul, he was a union man. Always talking about fairness, about the working stiff getting a fair shake. He believed in getting involved, going to the meetings, making your voice heard. I admired that. I finished secondary school, but things were tight. A girl needs to work, you know? So I didn't follow his path exactly. I worked hard, cleaned houses mostly, then I got lucky, helping an older señora manage her properties. It’s good money, reliable. Enough to give my children a decent education, better than I had. 

Now, I’m comfortable. Not rich, mind you, but comfortable. A small house of my own, a little savings. And honestly, when you *have* something to lose, you start paying attention. Not to the grand speeches, not to the promises, but to what actually affects my life. Taxes, the cost of groceries, healthcare…that’s what matters. I see the young people protesting, and I understand their frustrations, truly. But it all feels so…distant sometimes. The politicians, they live in another world. They don’t know what it’s like to stretch a peso.

I follow the news, of course. Radio Primero is my favorite. But I don't get worked up about it all the time. Too much shouting, too much blaming. It feels like everyone’s just trying to get their own piece of the pie. I believe in responsibility, in working for what you have. But you also need a government that doesn’t make things harder than they need to be. So, yes, I *am* interested. I have to be. It affects my family, my little bit of security. It’s just… exhausting.

profile_89261_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, politics. It’s always been…present. My father, God rest his soul, he was *involved*. Not a politician himself, mind you, but he managed our estancia, and that meant dealing with the intendente, the agricultural ministry, always needing permits, subsidies, trying to get a fair price for our wool and beef. He always said a good proprietor had to know what was happening in Montevideo, had to understand the laws being made. I learned a lot just listening to him, even if I wasn't formally educated in those things. I finished secondary school, which was good for a woman of my time, enough to keep the books and manage the household accounts. 

Now, I don’t go to rallies or anything like that. Too much shouting, too much…mess. But I *do* pay attention. I read *El País* every morning with my mate, and I listen to Radio Carve when I’m preparing the evening meal. It’s important to know what's happening with the economy – the price of fertilizer, the exchange rate, what the government is doing to help or hinder agriculture. My son, he’s taken over most of the day-to-day running of the estancia now, but I still oversee things, and I need to know if his decisions are being made with a clear understanding of the political climate.

It’s changed so much over the years, of course. The Tupamaros, the dictatorship…terrible times. We were fortunate, we weren't targeted directly, but the uncertainty, the fear… it impacted everything. Now things are calmer, but there’s still corruption, and the politicians seem more interested in fighting amongst themselves than in actually governing. Still, a responsible citizen must be aware. A good landowner *must* be aware. It’s not about liking it, it’s about protecting what you've worked for, for your family, and for Uruguay. So I wouldn't say I enjoy it, but I certainly wouldn’t ignore it.

profile_89280_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Honestly, politics… it’s always *there*, isn’t it? Like the humidity in summer. You can’t escape it, and it affects everything. My father was a lawyer, a very respected man in Montevideo. My husband, Ricardo, he’s an engineer. He tends to be more focused on the practical, on building and fixing. He finds the constant maneuvering and posturing tiresome. And I understand that, truly. But I feel like if people like us – educated, comfortable, able to provide for our families – don’t pay attention, things can easily drift. The parties… they're all flawed, of course. The Blancos, the Colorados, the Frente Amplio… all have their issues, their corruption, their internal conflicts. But dismissing it all as just “politics” feels… irresponsible.

We've been fortunate. We live in Pocitos, our children went to good schools, we've travelled. But I see the difficulties facing Uruguay, the economic pressures, the rising cost of living. I see how hard it is for the younger generation to find good opportunities. And I believe, even if it’s a small belief, that being engaged, being informed, is the least I can do. It’s not about blind loyalty to any party; it's about holding them accountable. It's about ensuring that Uruguay continues to move, however slowly, towards a more just and equitable society. So, while it's often disheartening, I *do* follow things. I read the newspapers, listen to the radio, and discuss issues with friends and family. It’s a duty, I suppose. A tiring one, but a duty nonetheless.

profile_89287_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, politics. It's always been…complicated. My father, he was a union man, always talking about fairness, about the working man getting a fair shake. The promises broken, the corruption…it aged him terribly. 

I finished secondary school, but there wasn’t money for university. I went straight to work, an office job at the cooperative. Good, steady work. It’s not glamorous, but it pays the bills, and I’ve got my pension building. I have a family, grandchildren now. My concerns are practical – making sure they have opportunities, that the prices don’t keep going up on everything, that the healthcare doesn't fall apart. 

Honestly, I try not to get too worked up about politics anymore. It feels like nothing ever really changes, and the people at the top don't care about someone like me. They say one thing during elections, then do another once they’re in power. Still, I follow the news a little, just to know what’s happening. You have to, don't you? It affects everything. But actively *interested*? No. It’s more like… resigned acceptance, with a little bit of hope that maybe, just maybe, things could be a little better for my grandchildren. It's exhausting, keeping up with it all.

profile_89288_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, politics. Always politics. Eighty-five years I’ve lived, and it feels like it’s *always* been politics causing trouble, or promises never kept. My father, he was a *porteño*, came from Buenos Aires, worked the docks here in Montevideo. Tough man, worked hard. He always said, “Don’t trust politicians, they’re all the same.” And you know, he wasn’t wrong. I finished school, secondary, but there wasn’t much opportunity for a girl then, not really. Ended up working in a *fonda*, serving food, cleaning, the usual. Saw all sorts come and go – workers, businessmen, even a few politicians now and then, flashing their money and talking big. 

It’s not that I don’t *care* what happens, you understand. Of course I do. I worry about my grandchildren, about the price of things going up, about the hospitals… but I’ve learned it doesn't matter much what *I* think. The rich get richer, the poor struggle, and the politicians… they just talk. They make promises during elections, then forget about us. My husband, God rest his soul, he was in the union. He believed in fighting for what’s right, but even he got discouraged after a while. He said it was like pushing water uphill.

I read the newspaper, I listen to the radio, I hear the gossip. But truly getting *involved*? Joining marches, arguing with strangers? No. Too much trouble. Too much disappointment. It's exhausting, and after all these years, I’ve just… resigned myself. I focus on my family, my garden, my *mate*. Those are the things I can control. Those are the things that matter. It’s better to keep your head down and survive, that’s what I’ve always believed.

I follow things generally, read *El País* and *Búsqueda* religiously, and I listen to Radio Carve when I’m driving. But I don’t get *involved* anymore. Those things feel…productive.

I believe in stability, in gradual improvement. Not these dramatic swings and constant upheaval. My friends, the ones who are really passionate, they get so upset, so stressed. I prefer peace of mind. I’ve earned it, after all these years. It’s not that I don’t *care* about the country, it's just that I have other priorities. My grandchildren, my garden, my volunteer work at the church… these are the things that truly matter.

profile_89304_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Honestly, politics… it’s always *there*, isn’t it? Like the humidity in summer. You can’t escape it, and sometimes it feels suffocating. My father was a lawyer, very involved in the Blanco party during the turbulent times. He believed in civic duty, in participating, in trying to steer things. I saw the toll it took on him, the endless meetings, the compromises, the disillusionment. I chose a different path, focused on education, on fostering critical thinking in my students. 

I’ve always believed in a strong, stable Uruguay, one that respects its traditions but isn’t afraid to move forward. We’ve had enough chaos in our history. But these days… it feels like the politicians are more interested in power struggles than in actually *governing*. The constant bickering, the accusations, the lack of long-term vision. It’s exhausting. 

I read the newspapers, of course, I keep up with current events. One has to. But I find myself increasingly cynical. I donate to charities, I volunteer at the cultural centre, I try to contribute to society in ways that feel… tangible. Direct. Politics feels so distant, so removed from the everyday lives of people like me, like my friends. It feels like a game played by a certain class, disconnected from the realities of most Uruguayans. I’m not apathetic, not exactly. I simply find myself… disengaged. It's draining to pay attention when it feels like little changes. My husband would have been very involved, he always was, but he passed some years ago, and I… well, I've let it slide.

I finished secondary school, which was good for a woman in my time, but I never went to university. I worked, always working. Honestly, it feels like no matter who's in power, things stay pretty much the same for people like me. The prices go up, the *pesos* lose value, and you just keep working harder to get by. I worry about my grandchildren, that they’ll have to struggle as much as we did, but getting angry or involved doesn't seem to change much. It’s exhausting. I keep up with the basics – who the president is, if they’re raising taxes – but I don't spend hours debating it. I have my rosary, my family, and that's enough.

profile_89319_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Look, politics… it's always *there*, isn't it? Like the flies buzzing around the cows in summer. My grandfather always said, "Don't bother with politicians, they're all the same – promise you the moon and deliver dust." And honestly, seeing how things are, it's hard to disagree. We work the land, my family has for generations. Long hours, hard graft. What does any politician *really* know about that? They talk about the economy, about big things happening in Montevideo, but what about the price of fertilizer going up again? What about the rains being late, threatening the whole harvest?

I finished secondary school, which is more than a lot around here do, but it wasn't about learning to *change* things, it was about getting a certificate. It’s about survival, really. My father expects me to eventually take over the small *chacra*. That's the life, the duty. Politics feels distant. It feels like something for people who aren't worrying about whether the cattle have enough to eat. 

I hear talk, of course. At the local *pulpería*, over a mate. Everyone complains about the taxes, the cost of things. But it rarely feels like talking actually *does* anything. It's just… venting. Besides, even if a politician *did* listen, would they actually care about a small farm like ours? They seem to always be focused on the big *estancias*, the ones that really have money. Still, ignoring it completely feels foolish. You have to be aware, at least, of what they're up to, so they don't take more than they should. It’s not that I enjoy it, though. It mostly just makes me frustrated.

profile_89335_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Look, politics… it’s always been something *other* people deal with, you know? The families with the big houses, the lawyers, the ones who went to university. My old man, he worked the land his whole life. Didn’t have time for speeches and promises. He said politicians are all the same – they tell you what you want to hear to get your vote, then forget about you the minute the election is over. I’ve done alright for myself, though. Been working as a driver for the wealthier families in Pocitos for twenty years now. Good money, reliable. I send money home to my mother, help my sister with her kids. That’s what matters. Keeping a roof over your family’s head, making sure they’re fed. 

I hear the chatter, of course. The señora I drive sometimes, she’s always talking about the parties, the latest scandal. Honestly, it just gives me a headache. It feels… distant. Like it doesn’t really affect my day-to-day. The price of beef at the market, that affects me. Whether my car needs a new tyre, that affects me. Not who’s arguing in Montevideo. Still, you gotta be aware, right? Know what’s going on, so they don’t pull the rug out from under you. But actually *getting involved*? No. Too much hassle. Too many lies. I’d rather focus on providing for my family. It’s just… I don't really have the time or the head for all that. I'm not unconcerned, but it isn't something I actively follow.
