profile_89336_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Honestly, politics. It’s…exhausting. My father was a lawyer, a very respected man in Montevideo, and he always said a citizen *had* to be informed, to participate. I tried, I really did. I studied political science at the Universidad, intending to go into public service. But what I saw… the constant maneuvering, the deals made behind closed doors, the focus on personalities instead of actual solutions… it disillusioned me quickly. 

Now, I work as a systems analyst for a private bank. It’s a good life, comfortable. My husband, Gonzalo, is an architect, also doing well. We've built a stable life for our children, sent them to good schools, even managed to help my sister with her business. That’s what matters – building something solid, something *real*. 

I still read the papers, of course. One has to be aware of what's happening. But I've learned to be incredibly pragmatic. I don’t believe in grand ideologies anymore. I’m much more concerned with practical things - the economy, maintaining a decent standard of living, ensuring my children have opportunities. 

I worry about the direction the country is taking, certainly. The constant fluctuations, the inflation… those things affect us all. But I don't believe endlessly debating abstract principles will *solve* anything. It requires competence, sound financial planning, and a long-term vision, things sadly lacking, it seems. Participating in rallies or getting involved in party politics? No, thank you. I focus on what I can control – my family, my career, and contributing to our community in small, meaningful ways. It feels more productive, less… futile.

I admire that, I really do. But for me, it’s more about understanding the system so I can navigate it effectively, if that makes sense. Protecting what we have, maybe improving things within the existing framework. Radical change? I'm not sure that's the answer. Stability is important. My parents worked hard for what they have, and I’d like to maintain that. It’s not that I *don't* care, it's just… I'm not actively seeking out political news or getting involved in protests. I’m focusing on my studies and trying to figure out what I want to do with my career. It just feels like a lot, you know? A constant headache.

I follow the news, of course, to stay informed, read *El País* regularly, and I have opinions. But to be actively *interested* in the political maneuvering? I’d much rather channel my energy into something concrete. But truly understanding it and *enjoying* engaging with it are two very different things. It feels…saturated. Filled with posturing and promises that rarely materialize. I see the effect on businesses, the constant shifting regulations, the bureaucratic hurdles. It impacts my work, certainly – I’m a project manager for a construction firm – but it’s rarely…inspiring. 

I follow the major news, of course. I need to. And I vote, naturally. I usually lean towards the more centrist, pragmatic parties. The Broad Front can be a bit too idealistic for my taste, and the traditional parties…well, they have their issues with corruption. But to say I actively *seek out* political discussion? No. I’d rather focus on my work, my family, maybe a weekend *asado* with friends. I worry about the direction the country is going, the economy, the increase in insecurity…but dwelling on it constantly just makes me anxious. It’s a necessary evil, keeping abreast of things, but it’s far from a passion.

It’s not that I don’t care about Uruguay, it’s just… I’ve learned not to expect much from the people running things.

profile_89367_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ugh, politics. Honestly? It feels like it’s all just talk, and very little *doing*. My parents, they always followed it closely, arguing about the Blanco and Colorado parties, who promised what… it never seemed to change much for us, you know? We're not rich, we're not poor, just… working. My dad was a mechanic, my mom cleaned houses. I finished high school, thankfully, but university is expensive. Now I sell clothes at the shopping in Punta del Este – seasonal work, mostly, but it pays the bills. It’s hard work standing all day, dealing with tourists… sometimes rude tourists. 

It’s not that I think it’s *wrong* to be interested in politics, it’s just… it feels distant from my life. What do these politicians know about struggling to make rent? About worrying if your abuela will be okay when the winter comes? They promise things, but I see the same problems every year: prices going up, jobs being scarce, the buses always late. I try to focus on my own life, on making enough to help my family, on maybe saving a little to get a small place of my own someday. 

I *should* probably pay more attention, my mother would say. But truthfully, after a long day trying to convince someone to buy a shirt they don't need, the last thing I want to do is listen to politicians making promises they likely won't keep. It just feels… exhausting. I try to stay out of it, honestly. I just want things to be a little bit easier, for everyone.

profile_89368_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Look, politics… it’s always *there*, isn’t it? Like the humidity in summer. You can't escape it. My grandfather, he was a sindicalista – a union man – always talking about the struggle, about derechos, about getting a fair shake for the workers. My father, he… he tried to stay out of it. Said it just brought trouble. I see both sides. 

I finished university, did a postgrado in industrial engineering. Thought I'd be designing things, making things *better*. Ended up doing quality control at the bottling plant. Good, honest work, but… it’s still just work. Still subject to the whims of the owners, to the government regulations, to the price of everything going up and my wages staying the same. 

Honestly, it feels like whichever party is in power, it doesn't really change much for a man like me. They make promises, they talk a good game, but in the end, they're all looking out for themselves and their friends. The big landowners, the importers… they always win. 

I follow the news enough to know what’s happening – the economy is the biggest worry, always. Inflation is killing us. Are they going to control it? Will my purchasing power keep eroding? That’s what concerns me. But I don’t go to rallies, I don’t join parties, I don’t really *believe* in politicians. I believe in hard work, in providing for my family, and hoping things don’t get much worse. A man has to focus on what he can control, you know? So, I’m aware, I pay attention when I have to, but… truly *interested*? No. More like resigned.

Honestly, I think most politicians are…disconnected. They don’t understand what it’s like for people like me, working to make ends meet. profile_89404_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, politics. Seventy years I’ve been breathing Uruguayan air, and seventy years I've seen politicians come and go, promising the world and delivering… well, not much different than before, usually. My father, he was a *gauchito*, worked the land. Didn’t have much time for politics, didn’t *need* much time. He figured hard work and keeping your head down was the best way to get by. I finished secondary school – not university, that was for the rich kids, even back then – and became a mechanic. A good, honest trade. I fixed cars, I provided for my family. 

I voted, of course, always have. It’s our duty as citizens, right? But I’ve learned not to expect miracles. They all talk a good game, these politicians. They talk about the *pueblo*, the people, but they seem more interested in looking after their own. There's always corruption, a little here, a little there. It's disheartening. 

Now, I keep an eye on things. I read *El País* most days, listen to the radio. You have to know what’s going on. But I don't get *involved*. No marches, no rallies. Too much shouting, not enough doing. I worry more about the price of beef and whether my grandson will be able to find a decent job. Those are the things that matter, the things that affect a working man. It’s… exhausting, really. All the promises, the debates, the bickering. It rarely translates into anything tangible for someone like me. So, interested enough to know what's happening, but not enough to get my hopes up, or get deeply involved.

I see the politicians on TV, looking all important, and I think, what do *they* know about struggling to make rent? About worrying if you’ll have enough for milk for your little brother? They talk about the economy, but they don't talk about the price of onions going up. It all feels so distant. I try to keep my head down and focus on getting through the day. I believe in hard work and family. I believe in helping your neighbor when they need it. But believing in politicians? That’s harder. It’s not that I don’t *care* about the country, it's just… I feel powerless. It's easier to focus on what I can control, on making sure my family is okay. Honestly, I'm tired of being disappointed. It’s not that I ignore it completely, you hear things, you see things… but I don’t actively *seek* it out. It just adds to the stress.

profile_89481_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ugh, politics. Honestly, it’s…complicated. My family, we’ve always been comfortable. Papa built a really successful import-export business, and Mami handles the family investments now. We don’t *need* to worry about things like potholes or public schools – we send the children to the British school, and Papa makes sure the roads around our neighbourhood are kept up. It’s not that I’m ignorant, I studied political science at Universidad de la República, you know? But it felt… disconnected. So much arguing, so little *doing*. 

Everyone in my circle talks about the economy, of course. Keeping up the value of our properties, ensuring our businesses thrive. That feels… practical. Talking about social programs or land reform just feels like endless complaining. It’s not that I don’t care about people, truly. It’s just… there’s a certain level of responsibility that comes with having resources, and that means protecting what you’ve built. It’s about stability, about ensuring Uruguay continues to be a safe and attractive place for investment. 

I follow things generally – the news, the elections – but I don't feel *compelled* to actively participate. I go to the occasional charity gala, donate when I can, but marching in the streets? Joining a party? No. That feels… uncouth. And frankly, exhausting. I’d rather focus on my interests - dressage, interior design, travelling. Things I can actually *control* and enjoy. It’s not apathy, it’s… prioritisation.

Everyone complains about the politicians, of course. My *abuela* used to say they’re all thieves, and honestly, it’s hard to argue with that. My husband, Ricardo, he gets more worked up about it. He’s always reading the newspapers, yelling at the radio. Says we need to fight for our rights. But what can *I* do? I work all day, then come home and cook and clean and look after the kids. I don’t have the energy to go to protests or write letters. It feels like it wouldn’t make a difference anyway. The rich always look after the rich. It's just… exhausting thinking about it. It’s easier to focus on getting through the day, making sure my children are fed and have a roof over their heads. That's real life. Politics feels like a different planet.

profile_89485_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Look, politics… it’s always *there*, isn’t it? Like the humidity in summer, you can’t escape it. My grandfather, he was a sindicalista – a union man – very involved. He believed in fighting for the working man, for *nosotros*. My father, he mostly kept his head down, worked hard at the tannery, saw how little good all the arguing did. I finished my degree, but a degree in history doesn’t exactly open doors here. Ended up working with my cousin, fixing cars. Good, honest work. 

Honestly, I’m tired of promises. Every election, they say the same things: more jobs, better healthcare, lower prices. It rarely changes for people like me. I see the politicians on TV, fancy suits, talking about things that don’t touch my life – inflation, international trade… while my electricity bill keeps going up. I read the newspapers when I have time, mostly to see what’s happening with the price of beef and if they’re raising taxes again. I vote, of course. It's what my grandfather would want. But I don't expect miracles. I’m more interested in getting through the week, making sure my family’s okay. I follow things enough to know who’s screwing us over the most, but deeply *interested*? No. It just feels… exhausting. It feels like all the same faces, the same games, and very little real change. I need to focus on what I *can* control, not what some politician promises.

profile_89486_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Politics… *uff*. Honestly, it feels like watching a football match where both teams are losing. All shouting and pushing, and in the end, nothing changes for us, the ones actually working. My father always said to keep your head down and work hard, that's how you get by. He was a *tornero* – a lathe operator – all his life. Solid work, honest work. I finished secondary school, did okay, but there weren't opportunities for more. Ended up in construction, then driving a delivery truck. Now I'm mostly doing odd jobs, helping people with repairs, things like that. 

What do these politicians know about getting up before dawn to earn a living? They talk about the economy, about growth… but my grocery bill doesn’t care about growth! It just keeps going up. They promise things, always promises, but the *costo de vida* – the cost of living – always finds a way to eat it all up. My neighbors, we talk. We complain about the same things: the buses are always late, the healthcare system is falling apart, the *intendente* only appears during elections.

I used to pay a bit more attention, when my kids were young. Wanted to make sure things were a little better for them. But now… feels like it’s all just the same old story, different faces. It's exhausting, honestly. Better to focus on what I can control: fixing the leaky roof, helping my grandson with his homework, making sure we have enough for *asado* on Sunday. It's easier that way. Safer. I don’t trust them, any of them. They’re all looking out for themselves.

It's not that I *don’t* care. Of course I care! I want things to be better for my son, for everyone. I want a decent wage, affordable healthcare, schools that actually have the resources they need. But it feels like they don't listen to us, the working people. They talk *about* us, but they don't actually *listen*. Carlos says we have to stay informed, that if we don't participate, things will only get worse. He’s probably right. But truthfully, it’s hard to feel hopeful when you see the same problems year after year. I read the headlines when I have a moment, and I hear things from my coworkers, but I don’t go seeking out information. It just stresses me out. I feel like a small cog in a very large, very broken machine. Still, I do vote. Always. My mother would haunt me if I didn't.

I remember him getting excited about Frei, thinking things would change. They didn’t, really. Just different faces, same problems.

I finished secondary school, learned a trade – carpentry, good honest work. Keeps the hands busy, puts food on the table. I’ve seen governments come and go, promises made and broken. The Blanco, the Colorado… they all seem to look out for themselves first, and us, well, we’re just expected to get on with it. 

Honestly, it feels like no matter who’s in power, the price of things keeps going up, the peso keeps losing value, and the rich get richer while we struggle. I follow things a little, enough to know who's making the mess, but getting *involved*? No. Too much shouting, too little doing. It just makes my blood pressure rise. I concentrate on my work, on my family. That’s where my energy needs to be. I’m 67 now, I've seen enough to know that getting your hopes up is a recipe for disappointment. Better to just focus on what you can control.

I read the newspapers, listen to the radio…you have to know what’s going on. But I don’t get involved. That’s what matters. A man needs to focus on what he *can* control, not what the politicians say they will do. I'm not a naive man. I've seen too much. So, I’m aware, I know what's happening…but I wouldn't say I enjoy it. It’s a necessity, not a pleasure.

profile_89546_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, politics. It’s always been…present. My father was a mechanic, a good one, but involved in the sindicato, the union. Always talking about fair wages, the rights of the worker. I studied engineering at the Universidad, a good school, but I ended up working in the talleres, the workshops, fixing things, building things. A practical man, that's me.

I’ve seen governments come and go. Seen promises made, and broken. The Blanco, the Colorado…they all talk the same. They say they’re for the gente, the people, but it’s usually just talk. They look after their own, that's the truth. 

I used to follow it all more closely, read Clarín, listen to the radio. But what’s the point, really? Things don't change much, do they? The rich get richer, and we keep working. Still, you have to be aware. You have to know what they're up to, so they don't take everything from you. My pension isn't much, you understand. Every peso counts. So, I keep an ear out. It’s a responsibility, I think, to know what's happening, even if it’s frustrating. I’m not running to join a party or anything. Just…observing. Keeping watch. A man needs to.

My brother, he had a bit of luck with a technical course, but I ended up doing what a lot of people around here do – working in a factory. It’s honest work, but it's hard on the body. What I care about is being able to pay the bills, make sure my kids are fed, and maybe, just maybe, afford a little something nice now and then. 

I hear about corruption, about them lining their own pockets, and it just makes me feel… powerless. It’s frustrating. You work and work, and they just seem to take and take. I try to keep my head down and focus on my family. It’s easier that way. To be honest, getting involved feels like a waste of energy. I need that energy for work, for my children. A little hope would be nice, but I've learned not to expect much.

I read the newspapers, the *El País* mostly. I listen to the radio while I'm getting ready for work. But I don’t go to rallies or join parties. It just feels… draining. My brother, he's more involved. He goes to the union meetings, yells a lot. He says we gotta fight for our rights. But honestly? I’m tired. Tired of fighting, tired of hoping. I just want a quiet life, a little bit of peace. It feels like they’re all the same, anyway. Stealing from us, one way or another. I just focus on my work, my family… that’s enough for me. profile_89626_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Politics… *ay*, it’s a complicated thing. I spent my life working at the post office, sorting letters, seeing everything that comes and goes. You learn a lot about people that way, about what they worry about, what they hope for. And it's always the same complaints, really. The cost of *mate*, the buses running late, the politicians promising everything and delivering nothing. 

I went to the university, studied history, a long time ago now. Thought I’d make something of myself, perhaps a teacher. But my father fell ill, and I needed to work. A good, solid job. The post office was that. It provided. Stability. You don't get that from politics.

It’s not that I *dislike* the idea of a good government, mind you. Of course not. A man wants to feel secure, wants to know his pension will be there when he needs it. My generation, we saw so much upheaval. The dictatorships… terrible times. So, I understand the need for order, for someone to be in charge. But I've also seen how easily that order can turn into oppression. 

I used to pay more attention, read the papers, listen to the radio. But after a while, it all feels the same. Empty promises, corruption, arguments that never get solved. It's exhausting. Better to focus on my family, my garden, a good cup of coffee. Things you can actually *control*. 

My son, he's very involved. Always talking about parties and candidates. I admire his passion, truly. But I tell him, "Son, politics is a messy business. Don’t expect too much." It’s better to be realistic. I've seen enough in my life to know that. I follow things generally, enough to know when things are truly going badly, but I don’t get deeply involved. It just… it feels like a waste of energy at my age. A man needs to rest.

profile_89627_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Politics… *sigh*. At my age, you've seen enough of it to be…weary. I was a structural engineer, you understand. Precise calculations, demonstrable results. That’s a world I understood. This…this is all maneuvering, promises that rarely materialize, and too much focus on personalities instead of practical solutions. My father was involved, briefly, in the Blanco party when I was a boy. He quickly grew disillusioned. He said it was all “much ado about nothing,” a phrase that stuck with me. 

I have a comfortable life. My pension is decent, my children are well-educated and established—one a doctor in Montevideo, the other running a small vineyard in Canelones. I don't *need* to be involved in the daily squabbles. I read *El País* each morning, of course, to stay informed. One can’t entirely ignore it. But truthfully, I find it increasingly frustrating. The constant left-right bickering, the populism… it feels so unproductive. 

We need stability, solid infrastructure, a commitment to education and technological advancement. Things that actually *improve* life for everyone, not just talking points for the next election. I’ve donated to a few causes over the years—the hospital in Pocitos, a scholarship fund at the Universidad—but direct action isn't my style. I prefer to focus on what I can control: my family, my garden, a good book. It’s simply exhausting to get worked up about things I feel largely powerless to change. It’s not that I’m apathetic, it’s…pragmatic. A man reaches a certain age and realizes some battles aren't worth fighting.

profile_89628_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Politics… it’s always been a presence, hasn’t it? My father was a lawyer, a good one, not one of those ambulance chasers, but a man who believed in the rule of law and civic duty. He instilled in me a certain respect for the process, for the institutions. I studied engineering at the Universidad de la República, a solid education. It teaches you to analyze, to look at problems systematically. And politics *is* a problem, always has been. 

For years, I believed in building a stable life, providing for my family. A good career, a comfortable home in Pocitos, educating the children… that was the focus. I always voted, of course. Partido Colorado, mostly. Pragmatism, you see. The Blanco party has its moments, but historically, they lean too heavily into nostalgia, into trying to recreate a past that isn't coming back. The Frente Amplio… well, they mean well, but their policies, the emphasis on social programs, inevitably lead to economic instability. We’ve seen it happen repeatedly.

Lately, though, it's become… frustrating. The constant scandals, the petty infighting, the lack of long-term vision. It feels like they’re all just jockeying for position, not actually *governing*. And the influence of Venezuela… that’s deeply concerning. We’ve worked hard to build a stable, relatively prosperous country, and it feels like they’re willing to risk it all on ideological fantasies. 

I read *El País* and *Búsqueda* religiously, try to stay informed. But it's exhausting. Truly exhausting. It’s not that I’ve given up, exactly. I still feel a responsibility to be aware. But the level of cynicism and corruption… it weighs on you after a while. I wish they would focus on things that matter – infrastructure, education, encouraging private enterprise – instead of these endless ideological battles. So, interested, yes… but increasingly disillusioned. It's a duty, not a pleasure, these days.

I’m not a *passive* citizen, mind you. I read *El País* religiously, I follow the debates, I discuss things with my friends at the Club Uruguay. No. I’m more interested in my grandchildren, my garden, a good bottle of Tannat. Those are the things that truly matter at this stage in life. It’s exhausting to get worked up over something that often feels… beyond your control. I do my civic duty, vote, keep informed, but actively pursuing political engagement? That’s for the younger generation, let them have at it. I've had my fill.

Honestly, it feels like nothing *ever* changes. Politicians promise things, they make speeches…and then things just stay the same. The cost of living keeps going up, and the young ones struggle to find good work. I worry about my grandson, Pablo. He’s a good boy, wants to be a mechanic, but the opportunities…they’re limited. 

I read the newspaper when I can, mostly the local news. I need to know if the buses are running on time, if the price of beef is going up again. profile_89642_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, politics. It's always been…present, hasn't it? Even when you try to avoid it, it finds you. My father, he was a union man, a *sindicato*. He believed in fighting for the workers, for fair wages. I remember him coming home, tired and heated, talking about strikes and bosses and the need for things to be just. I didn’t join him, mind you. A woman's place was often seen as… elsewhere. I did clerical work, mostly for the municipality, keeping records, filing papers. It wasn’t glamorous, but it was steady. You see a lot passing through those offices, though. Petitions, complaints, requests… you get a sense of what people are worried about. 

I went to secondary school, a *liceo*, which was good for a girl in my time. I learned to read and write properly, to do figures. But real understanding? That comes from *living*, from seeing how things are. I’ve seen governments come and go, promises made and broken. Seen the cost of living rise and fall, families struggle, young people leave for Argentina or Spain looking for work. 

Honestly, it feels like everything just… continues. The same problems, different faces. It's disheartening. And now, with everything so divided, so angry… all the shouting on the television… It’s exhausting. I pray for peace, for stability, for a little bit of decency. It’s important to be informed, to know what’s happening. But to be *interested*? To actively follow it all? It just feels like a recipe for unhappiness. I prefer to focus on my family, my garden, my faith. Those are the things that bring me comfort. Still, I do keep an ear out… you have to, to protect what you have.

He’s retired now, thankfully. I still give private lessons to keep a little money coming in. It's never enough, you know? The prices of everything keep going up, and the politicians just… talk. 

I follow things, of course. You have to. The economy worries me – my pension, what little my students’ families can afford for lessons. The insecurity is awful. And the corruption! It's always the same story, isn't it? People getting rich while the rest of us struggle. My mother always said, "Don't get involved, it's a dirty business." I tried to be optimistic when Lacalle Pou was elected, hoping for something different, but… it feels like more of the same. Empty promises.

I do vote, always. It feels like a duty, even if I’m not convinced it makes much difference. But deeply interested? No. It’s more… a resigned acceptance that you have to pay attention, lest things get even worse. It’s a weight, really. A constant worry. I’d much rather be tending to my garden or reading a good book. Something *real*.

profile_89657_gemma3:27b.think_process:

But I don’t go to rallies, I don’t donate to campaigns, and I certainly don’t waste time arguing with strangers on social media. It’s just… too disheartening. It feels like a game played by others, and we, the working people, are just spectators. I'm more interested in my grandchildren, in making a good *mate* and having a quiet evening with my husband. Those things feel… real. Tangible. Worth focusing on. I'm aware it sounds cynical, but after sixty years, you get a little weary.

profile_89658_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Honestly, politics… it's a necessary evil, isn’t it? My father was a lawyer, a very respected one, and always involved in civic duties. He instilled in me a sense of responsibility to be informed, to understand the mechanisms of government. I have a degree in Economics from the Universidad de la República, so I *do* understand the theory. And of course, with my husband being a doctor, we’ve always been comfortably situated, so we feel the effects of policy changes – tax increases, healthcare regulations, that sort of thing – quite directly. 

But truthfully, I find the current climate… disheartening. The constant bickering, the populism, the lack of real, substantive debate. It feels like everyone is shouting, and no one is listening. The Frente Amplio had its time, and while I appreciate their social programs, they really mismanaged the economy. Now we have the Nacionales trying to right the ship, but it’s slow going, and there's so much polarization. 

I read *El País* every day, of course, and I keep up with the major issues. I discuss things with my friends, most of whom are in similar professions and have similar concerns. We worry about the future, about our children's opportunities, about maintaining a decent standard of living. It’s not that I’m *disengaged*, exactly. It’s more that I’m… fatigued. I wish they would just *govern* properly, for once. I contribute to charities, I vote, I try to be a responsible citizen. But following the daily drama? It’s exhausting. It truly is. I find myself focusing on things I *can* control - my family, my garden, my book club.

profile_89661_gemma3:27b.think_process:

Said they all promised the same things and delivered nothing. 

I worked at the post office for thirty years. You see *everything* there, you know? All the bills, the letters… you get a sense of how people are struggling. And you hear the gossip, of course. The cost of living just keeps going up, and the young people, they’re leaving to find work elsewhere. 

I keep up a little, I read the Búsqueda newspaper when I can, and I listen to Radio Carve when I’m ironing. But honestly, it just feels… exhausting. It's hard to feel hopeful when it seems like no one is really listening to people like me. I just want a stable country, where my grandchildren can have opportunities. Is that too much to ask? It's not that I *don’t* care, it's just... I'm tired of being disappointed. I focus on my family, my garden, my rosary. Those things I can control.

profile_89688_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, politics. It’s always been…present, hasn't it? I finished my degree in accounting at the Universidad, a good education, and worked most of my life as a bookkeeper for a small transport company. After that, there was so much hope with the restoration of democracy. But then the economic crises came, one after another. The constant instability… it wears you down. You see the politicians making promises, and very little actually changes for people like us. 

I follow the news, of course. You can’t help it. But I’ve learned not to get too invested. Too many disappointments. They talk about ideals, but it’s often about personal gain. I’m not saying it’s unimportant, it’s just… exhausting. I’ve seen so many come and go, all talking the same talk. I believe in being a good citizen, in fulfilling your obligations, but I've lost faith in the system itself. I'm interested enough to know what's happening, to be aware, but I'm not one to get involved. It feels like shouting into the wind.

profile_89704_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Politics… it’s always been *there*, hasn’t it? A necessary evil, perhaps. My father was a lawyer, a good one, and always involved in the Partido Colorado. He believed in order, in stability, in a strong hand guiding the country. I received a good education – the Universidad de la República, a solid base – but I chose a different path. I ended up in estate management, overseeing the land and properties for a few families, well-established families. It’s skilled work, requiring knowledge of law, agriculture, finances… but it’s not exactly glamorous. I’m comfortable, though. Very comfortable. 

This constant upheaval… the Frente Amplio in power for so long, now a return to the Nacional Party… it all feels a bit exhausting. It’s not that I don’t care about Uruguay, of course I do. I want what's best for my grandchildren, for this country to remain a haven of relative peace and prosperity. But these endless debates, the posturing, the promises that rarely materialize... it's disheartening. 

I follow things generally. I read *El País*, scan the news online. I need to be aware of changes in land regulations, tax policies that might affect my clients. But I don't get *involved*. Too much noise, too much drama. The real work gets done by people like me, quietly managing things, ensuring the wheels keep turning. I attend the occasional social gathering where politics are discussed, offer a reasoned opinion when asked, but I avoid the rallies, the public displays. It’s… undignified, somehow. A gentleman observes, he doesn't shout. I’ve seen enough changes in my sixty-five years to know things rarely improve as quickly as people hope. A cautious approach, a pragmatic outlook… that’s what serves Uruguay best, not fiery rhetoric.

Honestly, it exhausts me. Politics. It used to feel…important, you know? When my father was involved with the Blanco party, back when things felt truly unstable. He believed in civic duty, in *participating*. I went to university—studied psychology, of all things—and even then, the political discussions were constant. We debated everything, really. But now? It feels like a constant stream of accusations and posturing. The parties seem more focused on squabbling than on actually governing for the good of Uruguay. 

I follow things, of course. I read *El País* every morning with my mate, and I listen to Radio Carve when I’m preparing dinner. You can’t *not* be aware. We’re a small country, what happens in the Palacio Salvo affects all of us, especially those of us who’ve worked hard to build a comfortable life. My husband, Rafael, he's an engineer, very pragmatic. He says the politicians just don’t understand the realities of running a business, of creating jobs. And he's largely right. 

I have my concerns, naturally. The rising cost of living worries me, especially for the younger generation trying to get ahead. And the insecurity… it's not what it used to be, thankfully, but it’s still a worry when my grandchildren are out. But I find myself increasingly disillusioned. It feels like my voice doesn't matter, that the system is rigged, and that true, honest work—building a family, contributing to the community, being a good neighbor—is more meaningful than engaging in the endless political games. I wish they’d focus on the economy, on education, on *results*. 

So, I’m aware, I worry, I observe… but truly *interested*? Not as much as I once was. It feels… draining.

It's not that it doesn't affect us, it does. But getting involved? No. Too much energy wasted on things that won’t change.

profile_89722_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, politics. It’s always been the same, hasn’t it? Promises, promises… and then nothing for people like me. I sell empanadas down by the Plaza Independencia, been doing it for nearly forty years. My husband, God rest his soul, he worked the docks. We never had much, but we had enough. Enough to raise the kids, enough to put a roof over our heads. 

It’s just…they talk about big things, these politicians. About the country, about progress. But what does that even *mean* for a woman trying to make a decent living selling food? They raise prices on the flour, on the beef, on everything, and suddenly *my* progress is going backwards. My little bit of savings doesn't go as far.

I voted for a while, you know? Thought maybe if I voted for the right person, things might be a little easier. But it never seemed to make a difference. The rich get richer, and the rest of us… we just keep selling empanadas. My daughter, she’s very involved now, always talking about protests and change. Good for her. I just don't have the energy for it anymore. It feels like shouting into the wind. Better to focus on keeping my little business afloat, keeping food on *my* table.

I read the newspaper sometimes, mostly just the gossip pages, if I’m honest. And I listen to the radio while I’m making the dough. But I don’t really follow all the details. Too much noise, too much arguing. It’s exhausting. It doesn’t really touch my life, not in a way I can see. It’s just…there.

So, I’m aware of politics, I *have* to be, but actively *interested*? That’s different. It feels like a waste of energy, to be frank. It’s more of a necessary evil I have to tolerate.

That taught me to analyze, to see the structures, the underlying mechanics of everything. And politics *is* a structure, isn't it? It’s more than just national politics, too. The situation in Brazil, Argentina… it all impacts us. Uruguay is a small country, reliant on stability. And stability requires understanding the forces at play, both here and abroad. I don’t participate in rallies, I don’t shout slogans. I prefer to observe, to assess, and to offer my opinion when asked by those I trust. A quiet, considered involvement, that’s always been my approach. It's… a responsibility, really. A duty born from privilege and education. Though these days, it's increasingly frustrating to watch the same mistakes repeated.

profile_89736_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Look, politics… it’s always *there*, isn’t it? Like the humidity in summer, or the price of beef going up. You can’t really escape it. My old man, he used to follow everything, every debate, every little scandal. Drove my mother mad. Me? I just try to get by. I work hard, I provide for my family, that’s what matters. I finished secondary school, good enough to get a job, not enough to become a lawyer or doctor. So I do what I do. 

It’s not that I don't *care*, exactly. It's just… what difference does it make, really? The parties all seem the same to me. Promises, promises. They talk about the economy, about jobs, about helping the *trabajador* like me, but things just… stay the same. Always a struggle. Always a little tighter each month. 

I read the newspapers when I have a moment, mostly to see the football scores, if I'm honest. Sometimes I overhear things at work, the guys talking about the latest news. I’ll listen, nod along, maybe offer an opinion if pressed, but I don’t actively *seek out* political information. It feels like a waste of energy, honestly. Energy I need to spend earning a living. I have responsibilities. Politics feels… distant, for other people. People with more time. More education. More…hope, maybe?

I think about my son, though. I want things to be better for him. More opportunities. But getting involved… it feels like shouting into the wind.

profile_89737_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, politics. It’s always been…present, hasn’t it? Growing up, my father, he always followed things closely. He worked at the post office, saw everything that came and went, heard all the gossip. He used to say a good citizen *had* to pay attention, know what was happening, or the wolves would come for you. He was a Blanco, through and through, but a decent man.

I finished school, not university, of course. We didn't have the money. Went straight to work as a secretary, and I’ve been doing it ever since. Mostly for the municipality. You see a lot when you work for the government, even at my level. Deals, promises… things never quite adding up. 

Honestly, it’s tiring. It feels like nothing *really* changes. One party comes in, makes a bit of noise, then it's the same old problems – the cost of things going up, the buses always late, my pension… always shrinking in real money. I read the newspaper, I listen to the radio, but it all feels so… distant. Like it’s happening to other people, not me. My concerns are simpler. Will I have enough for the month? Will my grandchildren be alright? Will the doctor have the medicine I need?

I used to think getting involved could make a difference, but I've seen too many good people get chewed up and spat out. It’s safer to keep your head down, work hard, and pray things don’t get much worse. It's not that I *don't* care. It’s just… exhausting. It feels like a spectator sport, and I’m tired of watching.

A little better, maybe, but not a revolution.

profile_89742_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Politics… hmm. At my age, you see a lot come and go. I was a young man during the turbulent times, the dictatorships. My father, a professor of history at the university, instilled in me a sense of civic duty, a belief in the importance of informed participation. But honestly? It’s all become so… cynical. The promises, the posturing, the corruption. It feels less about serving the *nation* and more about personal ambition. 

I worked my entire life as an administrator for the Banco de la República Oriental del Uruguay. A solid, respectable career. Not flashy, but honest work. I saw how things *should* be done, how systems are meant to function. The disconnect between that ideal and the reality… it’s disheartening. 

I follow the news, of course. You can't help but, living here. Read *El País*, listen to Radio Carve. It’s important to be aware. But I find myself increasingly frustrated by the lack of real solutions, the constant bickering. My wife, bless her soul, says I worry too much. She's probably right. It's easier to focus on family, on grandchildren, on a good *mate* and a quiet evening. Participating feels… exhausting. A waste of energy. But to completely disengage feels irresponsible. It’s a balance. A difficult balance. I'm past the age of marching in protests, that's for sure. I simply wish they’d focus on stability, on maintaining what little progress we've made. And perhaps, a little less drama.

profile_89744_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Politics. *Politics*. Honestly, at my age, one has seen so much… so much come and go. My father was a lawyer, a very respected man in Montevideo. He instilled in me a sense of civic duty, of course. I studied engineering at the Universidad de la República, a good education. We were always expected to be informed, to understand the machinations of government. It was part of being a responsible member of society. 

I've always been comfortable, thankfully. My family’s land has been in the same hands for generations, and my career allowed me to maintain a certain… stability. So, I’ve never *suffered* directly from poor governance. However, I’ve seen the instability, the coups, the economic downturns. The constant struggle between wanting progress and respecting tradition. 

These days? It feels… different. The parties seem more interested in squabbling than in actually governing. The constant appeals to emotion, the lack of reasoned debate… it’s disheartening. It’s not like the old days, when there was a certain level of decorum, even among opponents. And truthfully, after a lifetime of involvement—attending meetings, contributing financially, advising younger men—I find myself increasingly… detached. I follow the broad strokes, naturally. One must. But the details, the daily dramas… they simply don’t hold the same appeal. I prefer to spend my time with my grandchildren, tending to my garden, reading a good book. There are more fulfilling ways to spend one’s declining years than worrying about the latest political scandal. A man needs peace.

Honestly, it’s exhausting. It truly is. We're a comfortable family; we have a good life in Pocitos. We worry about maintaining that – good schools for the children, access to quality healthcare, the stability of the economy. I want policies that encourage responsible growth, not grandstanding and unsustainable promises. I read *Búsqueda* regularly to stay informed, but I find myself increasingly tuning out the noise. It’s not that I *don’t* care, it’s that I feel powerless to effect real change, and frankly, I have a business to run and grandchildren to spoil. Ricardo handles the truly heavy lifting now. He keeps me updated on the major things, and I’ll voice my opinion when asked, but I don’t actively seek it out. It’s a necessity to be *aware*, but truly *interested*? It feels like a luxury I can no longer afford, emotionally.

I’m not chasing fantasies.

profile_89768_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, politics. Honestly? It feels like a whole other world, doesn’t it? One that doesn’t really *see* us. I finished escuela secundaria, but there wasn’t much beyond that, you know? I work at the packing plant, same as my mother and grandmother before me. We just…work. We keep things going. Politics feels like it’s for the people in suits, the ones you see on TV talking about things that don’t affect my day-to-day. 

My husband, Miguel, he tries to follow it a bit. He says we *should* be aware, that they're always changing things that hit our pockets. But honestly, after a long day sorting fruit, I just want to relax with a mate and watch a novela. What difference does it make who’s in charge, really? Prices still go up, the buses are still crowded, and getting ahead feels impossible. 

It's not that I think it's *bad* to pay attention, it’s just… exhausting. I worry about my kids, about making enough to get them through school, about keeping a roof over our heads. That’s where my energy goes. I don’t have time to get worked up over promises that never seem to come true. I believe in working hard, in family, in getting by honestly. That's enough for me. It’s just… it feels distant. Like it happens *to* us, not *with* us.

profile_89769_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, politics. It’s always been…present, hasn’t it? Growing up, my father was a teacher, a *profesor* of history, and very involved in the sindicato, the union. He believed in fighting for what’s right, for the working man. A good man, but it cost him, always arguing, always frustrated. I understood the ideals, of course, studied sociology at the Universidad, even did a postgraduate on urban planning. I wanted to *do* something, make Montevideo a better place for everyone, not just the wealthy. 

But reality…reality is different. I ended up working as a technician for ANTEL, the state telecom company. A steady job, a pension, that’s what mattered. A good, honest living. Years of seeing promises broken, of seeing politicians line their own pockets while people struggled…it wears you down. It truly does. I still read the papers, of course, keep up with things, but the hope…the fire my father had? It’s dimmed. I worry about my grandchildren, about their future, about the economy, about healthcare. But getting involved, going to rallies, believing things will actually *change*? It feels…naive now. I focus on what I can control: my family, my small garden, helping my neighbours when I can. Politics feels distant, like a game played by people who don't understand our lives. It's hard to get excited about it anymore. It’s exhausting, honestly.
